title: Overloading and default arguments

canonicalExplanation.summary: Overload resolution forms a candidate set, checks viability, ranks conversions and templates, and selects one best function or reports ambiguity.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Calls with int and double select matching overloads without conversion, while a short argument may promote and create an unexpected choice. Your goal is to resolve overload calls and repair ambiguity in a small API. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Loops and range iteration; pf abstraction calls; Value, reference and const-reference parameters; Expressions, operators and conversions. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: Overload resolution forms a candidate set, checks viability, ranks conversions and templates, and selects one best function or reports ambiguity. This rule is applied to concrete C++ source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Implicit conversions, initializer lists, default arguments, templates, concepts, and hidden base overloads can produce surprising candidate rankings. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: C++ chooses the overload whose implementation seems most specific to the runtime value.

includes: Create coherent overload sets without ambiguous or surprising conversions.
Resolve overload calls and repair ambiguity in a small API.
excludes: Implicit conversions, initializer lists, default arguments, templates, concepts, and hidden base overloads can produce surprising candidate rankings.

outcomes: Resolve overload calls and repair ambiguity in a small API.

examples.title: Overloading and default arguments: defined case
examples.situation: Calls with int and double select matching overloads without conversion, while a short argument may promote and create an unexpected choice.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: Calls with int and double select matching overloads without conversion, while a short argument may promote and create an unexpected choice.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Overload resolution uses compile-time argument types and ranked conversions, not runtime values or function-body intent.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Overload resolution uses compile-time argument types and ranked conversions, not runtime values or function-body intent.

examples.title: Overloading and default arguments: boundary case
examples.situation: A derived class adds one overload and uses using Base::name to keep intended base overloads visible.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: A derived class adds one overload and uses using Base::name to keep intended base overloads visible.
Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Implicit conversions, initializer lists, default arguments, templates, concepts, and hidden base overloads can produce surprising candidate rankings.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: C++ chooses the overload whose implementation seems most specific to the runtime value.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Resolve overload calls and repair ambiguity in a small API.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: C++ chooses the overload whose implementation seems most specific to the runtime value.
misconceptions.correction: Overload resolution uses compile-time argument types and ranked conversions, not runtime values or function-body intent.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete CPP rules, explain why this belief fails: C++ chooses the overload whose implementation seems most specific to the runtime value.

analogy.example: A system of self-managing components can model Overloading and default arguments: each component protects an invariant and owns resources through typed interfaces and lifetime.
usefulFor: Connecting types, value semantics, invariants, generic interfaces, ownership, and deterministic cleanup
limitations: C++ overload resolution, templates, value categories, object lifetime, and undefined behavior have no exact physical equivalent.
Implicit conversions, initializer lists, default arguments, templates, concepts, and hidden base overloads can produce surprising candidate rankings.

recap.summary: You should now be able to resolve overload calls and repair ambiguity in a small API. Keep this boundary in view: Implicit conversions, initializer lists, default arguments, templates, concepts, and hidden base overloads can produce surprising candidate rankings.
